question_text: Under the 1996 HRSA patient definition guidance, which of the following is a required element for an individual to be considered a 340B patient?

answers.text: The individual must be uninsured
The covered entity must maintain a record of the individual as its patient
The individual must be a Medicaid beneficiary
The individual must be seen on-site at the covered entity

explanation: HRSA's patient definition requires: (1) established relationship, (2) care from a covered entity provider, and (3) records maintained. The patient need not be uninsured or on-site.

answers.text: A covered entity purchases the same drug from two manufacturers
A drug benefits from both a 340B discounted price AND a Medicaid rebate for the same unit
A patient fills the same prescription twice in one month
Two covered entities share the same contract pharmacy

explanation: A duplicate discount occurs when a covered entity purchases a drug at the 340B price AND the state Medicaid program also collects a manufacturer rebate for that same unit.

question_text: Under a Medicaid 'carve-out' approach, a covered entity:

answers.text: Bills Medicaid for 340B drugs and claims the rebate simultaneously
Excludes 340B drugs from Medicaid claims to avoid duplicate discounts
Carves out high-cost specialty drugs from 340B purchasing
Bills Medicare instead of Medicaid for all 340B drugs

explanation: Under carve-out, the covered entity does not use 340B-purchased drugs when billing Medicaid, thereby avoiding the prohibition on duplicate discounts.

question_text: What is drug diversion in the context of the 340B program?

answers.text: Purchasing drugs through unauthorized wholesalers
Dispensing 340B drugs to individuals who are not 340B-eligible patients of the covered entity
Substituting a generic drug for a brand drug without patient consent
Transferring drugs between two covered entity sites

explanation: Drug diversion is the transfer or sale of 340B-purchased drugs to ineligible individuals. It is explicitly prohibited by the 340B statute.

question_text: Which of the following entity types is prohibited from using 340B pricing for orphan drugs?

answers.text: Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs)
Children's hospitals enrolled as DSH hospitals
Ryan White grantees
Native Hawaiian health programs

explanation: Under the ACA, certain hospital types (children's hospitals, critical access hospitals, free-standing cancer hospitals, rural referral centers, sole community hospitals) cannot use 340B pricing for orphan drugs. FQHCs and Ryan White grantees have no orphan drug exclusion.

question_text: Split-billing software is primarily used to:

answers.text: Calculate the ceiling price for a given NDC
Track and separate 340B-eligible patient transactions from non-eligible ones to prevent diversion and duplicate discounts
Automatically submit Medicaid claims on behalf of covered entities
Generate OPAIS enrollment forms

explanation: Split-billing software helps covered entities identify 340B-eligible patients at the point of dispensing, ensuring 340B drugs are only purchased for eligible patients.

question_text: An established relationship with a 340B patient requires that the provider at the covered entity:

answers.text: Be a licensed pharmacist employed by the covered entity
Have clinical responsibility for the individual's care
Have seen the patient within the last 30 days
Refer the patient to an in-house pharmacy only

explanation: The HRSA patient definition requires the individual to receive health care from a provider who has clinical responsibility — not merely an administrative referral.

question_text: Which primary safeguard prevents duplicate discounts for Medicaid fee-for-service claims at contract pharmacies?

answers.text: Contract pharmacy audit reports submitted to HRSA quarterly
Covered entities maintaining inventory separation or virtual inventory tracking that excludes 340B claims from Medicaid rebate billing
Medicaid automatically waives rebate claims for covered entities
HRSA issues a blanket exemption for contract pharmacies

explanation: Covered entities must track which prescriptions are billed to Medicaid. When a 340B drug is billed to Medicaid FFS, the covered entity must ensure no manufacturer rebate is also collected on that unit.

question_text: Which of the following is a covered outpatient drug under the 340B program?

answers.text: A drug administered to a patient admitted as an inpatient
An FDA-approved prescription drug dispensed to an outpatient of the covered entity
An over-the-counter medication
A DESI drug found to be less than effective

explanation: 340B covered outpatient drugs are FDA-approved (or DESI-effective) drugs dispensed to outpatients. Inpatient drugs, OTCs, and DESI-ineffective drugs are excluded.

question_text: Under a Medicaid managed care carve-in arrangement, which additional step must a covered entity take to avoid duplicate discounts?

answers.text: Stop purchasing all drugs at 340B prices for MCO-covered patients
Implement controls so that claims for 340B-purchased drugs are flagged to prevent manufacturers from paying rebates on those same units
Submit all MCO claims through HRSA before billing the MCO
Obtain a waiver from the state Medicaid agency each calendar year

explanation: In carve-in states, the covered entity must flag 340B-purchased drug transactions so that the state/MCO does not also collect a manufacturer rebate on those units — preventing the duplicate discount.
